topic: The Axial Skeleton: The Rib Cage
intro: The Rib Cage consists of 12 pairs of curved bones that protect the heart and lungs.
notes: Sternum: The flat breastbone where ribs attach at the front.
Cartilage: Flexible tissue that allows the chest to expand during breathing.
Thoracic Vertebrae: The 12 bones of the spine where ribs attach at the back.
Floating Ribs: The last 2 pairs of ribs that do not attach to the Sternum.

hotspots.label: Ribs
hotspots.description:
The ribs form a cage around the chest. There are three types:
1. True Ribs (1-7): Attach directly to the sternum.
2. False Ribs (8-10): Attach to the cartilage of the rib above.
3. Floating Ribs (11-12): Do not attach to the front at all.
hotspots.examTip: During inhalation (breathing in), the ribs move UP and OUT to increase chest volume.

hotspots.label: Sternum
hotspots.description: A flat, dagger-shaped bone located in the center of the chest. It provides the front attachment point for the ribs via cartilage.
hotspots.examTip: It protects the heart and produces red blood cells in its marrow.

hotspots.label: Thoracic Vertebrae
hotspots.description: These are the 12 vertebrae of the mid-back. Every single rib is attached to a thoracic vertebra at the back.
hotspots.examTip: They provide stability for the rib cage.

hotspots.label: Costal Cartilage
hotspots.description: This is the smooth, elastic tissue that connects the ribs to the sternum. Unlike bone, it can stretch slightly.
hotspots.examTip: Function: It allows the rib cage to expand and contract so the lungs can fill with air.
